chapters.title: Bill Ford hands the wheel to an outsider
chapters.text:
It was not easy for Bill Ford Jr., Henry Ford's great-grandson, to step down and introduce Alan Mulally as the new CEO of Ford Motor Company in 2006. Driven by love and dedication to the company, he took that courageous step anyway.

The ability to change is the key to survival.

Despite being an outsider in Detroit, Alan Mulally had the credentials to address the crisis Ford faced. He had previously helped rescue Boeing after 9/11, so the chance to save another American icon seemed too good to pass up.

Little did he know that Ford's problems would make Boeing's look like a walk in the park.

As the new CEO, Mulally confronted:

• An internal crisis rooted in toxic company culture
• Family squabbles among Ford descendants
• Eroding customer perception of Ford products
• Product quality that lagged behind competitors

He insisted on teamwork, developed a recovery plan, and worked relentlessly — tracking progress through a weekly business review.

By 2011, Mulally had transformed Ford into the world's most profitable car company. How he accomplished that feat is still studied in business schools around the globe.

It is failure that is easy. Success is always hard. ~ Henry Ford

Follow along as we recount this phenomenal turnaround — how Ford returned to the top of the American auto industry through hard work and determination.

chapters.title: Henry Ford's quality obsession could not save competitiveness
chapters.text:
Henry Ford's vision was straightforward: build a widely available car large enough for a family yet small enough for an individual, using top-quality materials and skilled professionals.

He aimed to design simple, efficient vehicles priced so any working man could own one, enabling families to travel. He founded The Ford Motor Company on June 16, 1903, to reach that goal.

True to that vision, he created the Model T — a dependable car that revolutionized the industry. Vehicles were no longer reserved for the wealthy; anyone could own one. Ford's concept began to build momentum.

In January 1914, a wage hike to $5 daily — far above typical earnings — sparked one of the largest economic migrations since the California Gold Rush. Men left agricultural work and headed to Detroit. The industrial middle class was born.

With growing demand, Ford built the world's first moving assembly lines, cutting production time from 13 hours to 90 minutes.

However good you are, never be complacent.

Despite heavy investment in quality, Ford struggled with competitiveness. Larger personalities dominated the market while Ford clung to its initial concept. A destructive corporate culture corroded the company from within.

By 1925, the Model T cost $260 — roughly $3,000 today — with Ford manufacturing upward of 1.6 million cars annually. Yet that figure was still about 200,000 less than production two years earlier. Low prices could not halt declining Model T sales.

In 1927, Ford introduced the Model A, which saw phenomenal demand. Still, the company failed to build on that success for another five years, losing money annually without fresh products.

Over time, General Motors, Chrysler, and Japanese automakers beat Ford through constant improvement and new models.

chapters.title: One man's grip on power corroded the company
chapters.text:
Ford Motor Company relied not only on a single product but also on a single leader.

In the early days, Henry Ford managed his industrial empire with a tight grip. He recognized talent effectively yet often alienated executives as they gained influence.

Ford distanced himself from skilled leaders because he struggled to share power. He preferred compliant subordinates and brought in enforcers to maintain order.

Employees lived in fear of sudden dismissal, tailoring responses to what they believed management wanted to hear — regardless of truth.

The company's structure lacked transparency. Ford gathered information through informal channels, including spying, rather than standard financial analysis.

As the company shifted to manufacturing World War II bombers and jeeps, the War Department worried about such a poorly managed organization controlling vital industries. After Edsel Ford's death in 1943 removed the company's most rational voice, authorities considered intervention.

The US government directed Lieutenant Henry Ford II to leave military service and run the company in Dearborn. The senior Ford initially resisted his grandson's leadership, but by September 1945 he handed over control.

Despite early positive changes, Henry Ford II fostered executive rivalries that undermined company values. Managers prioritized personal careers over corporate success. The unhealthy culture kept the Ford family in control and blocked outside CEOs from meaningful change.

Learn to handle success as well as failure.

Ford displayed a remarkable ability to bounce back. Whenever defeat seemed certain, the company somehow rebounded. Yet its gravest enemy was success itself — after each crisis, complacency returned.

Thus, Ford faced many economic challenges across its 103-year history.

chapters.title: Booms, busts, and near-collapse
chapters.text:
During the Great Depression, Ford's refusal to cut production produced a $120 million loss. General Motors avoided losses by scaling production strategically and reducing workforce. Ford rebounded with the nation in the postwar boom.

In the 1970s, a new era of regulation began in the US — automakers had to improve safety and cut emissions. Those requirements raised prices as the country grappled with recession after Vietnam.

Rising fuel prices shifted consumer preference toward economical vehicles. Americans turned to Japanese imports offering superior mileage and reliability compared with many domestic models.

Ford also became entangled in costly legal battles. During this period, over 100 fatalities and nearly 2,000 injuries were linked to the Ford Pinto and other models because of defects in gear systems.

By the end of the decade, Ford's products were widely regarded as the worst on the road. ~ Bryce G. Hoffman

Fortunately, Henry Ford II's accumulated wealth, combined with layoffs and factory shutdowns after his 1979 resignation, kept operations alive in Dearborn.

A new executive group negotiated a cost-cutting contract with the United Auto Workers and invested in innovative models like the Taurus.

The hardest times carry the prospect of a breakthrough.

By 1983, Ford recovered with an unprecedented $1.8 billion profit. By 1986, earnings surpassed General Motors. In 1987, Ford's profits exceeded the combined earnings of all Japanese and European car manufacturers.

The SUV trend of the 1990s signaled another turnaround. Ford introduced the Explorer in 1990, rapidly becoming the world's top-selling SUV and one of its most profitable products.

In 1998, Ford's profits soared above $22 billion — a record for any automaker.

chapters.title: Family control forces an outside rescue
chapters.text:
Henry Ford was stubborn about keeping the company within the Ford family, partly out of fear of inheritance taxes.

To address that concern, he established the Ford Foundation in 1936. When the foundation later sold shares, Henry Ford II and his bankers crafted a distinctive share structure with Class A and Class B shares. Class A shares traded publicly on the New York Stock Exchange; Class B shares remained exclusively with the Ford family.

This setup guaranteed the family would always control 40% of voting power — enough to block major decisions or appoint a chairperson.

Those factors enabled Edsel Ford's youngest child, Bill Ford, to become chairman by the end of the 1990s.

Despite Bill's education, outside directors doubted his financial literacy matched his ideals. They split the CEO and chairman roles, pairing Bill with Jacques Nasser.

Two problems followed. First, Nasser and Ford were unwilling to share authority. Second, Nasser abandoned financial discipline once he became CEO. The two men pulled the company in opposite directions.

Asking for help is often the best way to overcome failure.

With family support, Bill Ford made a compelling case to remove Nasser as CEO and stop further damage. Like Henry Ford, he believed salvation required keeping control within the family.

But reviving the company was not easy. Despite his best efforts, Bill realized he needed a more competent hand as CEO. Looking outside the automotive industry, he found Alan Mulally.

Bill Ford's deep commitment allowed him to swallow his pride and step down from the CEO role.

chapters.title: Mulally rebuilds culture from the ground up
chapters.text:
When Alan Mulally became CEO in July 2006, Ford Motor Company was in dire straits.

Most vehicles were sold below production cost, and the board even considered merging with another automaker.

Mulally offered a compelling vision and executed relentlessly. He matched production with demand, shut down factories, and laid off thousands of workers. He also instituted his renowned weekly performance reviews and kept Ford out of the bailout package sought by General Motors and Chrysler.

Mapping out the rules and objectives is the first step to success.

The biggest driver behind Ford's turnaround was the weekly Business Plan Review held every Thursday. Mulally held separate follow-up meetings with necessary parties to review critical issues.

These meetings followed ten rules:

1. Value the people
2. Welcome everyone
3. Bright vision
4. Precise objectives
5. United plan
6. Actual data and facts
7. Suggest solutions
8. Cherish one another
9. Be resilient — hang in there
10. Have fun along the way

Mulally also shared four simple aspects of his business plan with directors:

• Restructure to stay profitable — pay attention to current demand
• Develop new products customers want and value
• Find resources and improve the balance sheet
• Work in harmony

Mulally's most efficient decision was revamping Ford's corporate culture. The changes would not have lasted if he had relied on a single blockbuster product.

That way, Mulally and his team pulled off one of the greatest comebacks in business history. As the rest of Detroit collapsed, Ford went from the brink of bankruptcy to the most profitable automaker in the world.

Did you know? Mulally designed a Matrix Organizational Structure for Boeing that he later adapted for Ford.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Despite limited auto industry experience, Mulally restructured Ford after decades of mismanagement. He adapted principles developed at Boeing to Ford's needs, making manufacturing more efficient, executives more collaborative, and products more functional.

He also rallied the United Auto Workers to join the fight to save American manufacturing. The contract signed with UAW changed the game. Ford family members held on to their stock instead of writing off the company — a decision that paid off handsomely.

Under Mulally's command, Ford allied with Japanese manufacturers Toyota and Honda to help keep the American automotive industry afloat.

Considered one of the greatest comebacks in business history, Ford became the most profitable automaker in the world.

Through Business Plan Review meetings, a clear strategy, and a healthier corporate culture, Mulally drove change and forced the company to face the realities of the American auto industry.

Alan Mulally redeemed America's last great industrial dynasty from extinction. Ford Motor Company now stands tall because of those actions.

Try this


• When facing a problem, define and attack the root of it.
• Map out a plan and strategy whenever you deal with something.
• Create a bright vision and set clear goals.
• Be relentless in your work.

Read it till the end?
